schools.summary: The opening of the providential journey — Jacob 'saw,' that is learned, there was corn in Egypt (the news spreading by the caravan trade, Gen 37:25, Ellicott; the corn his neighbours had brought home, Wesley); 'why do ye look one upon another?' (the brothers idle and irresolute, their father rousing them to act).

Calvin: Moses begins the account of how God drew Jacob's family into Egypt by hidden and unexpected methods of providence — the famine that threatens being the means of preservation and of the dreams' fulfilment.

themes: why do ye look one upon another?

schools.summary: Jacob's command to go down — what was his 'seeing' (v1) explained as hearing, 'I have heard that there is corn in Egypt'; the urgent charge 'get you down... and buy for us... that we may live, and not die' (the famine severe, life at stake, the only remedy Egypt's grain); masters of families must not only pray for daily bread but provide it with diligence (Wesley); the means of life lying in the hand of the brother they sold for dead.

themes: I have heard there is corn
that we may live, and not die

schools.summary: The descent of the ten — they obeyed their father and immediately went down, ten travelling together (Gill); Moses names them pointedly 'Joseph's ten brethren' (recalling the men who sold him, now going to the land where he rules); given the family's size and the famine, either their households were reduced or each took servants and beasts, that enough corn be carried home to be of use (Ellicott); the providential irony of the errand leading to Joseph.

Jacob's protective fear for Benjamin — called 'Joseph's brother' because he alone was Joseph's full brother, by both father and mother, the two sons of Rachel (Gill); kept back 'lest mischief befall him' (Jacob, having lost Joseph as he believed, clinging anxiously to the youngest and only remaining son of his beloved Rachel, his darling); the father's trembling love guarding the surviving brother, foreshadowing the very demand Joseph will make.

The famine reaching even Canaan — 'the sons of Israel' (the family of promise, reduced like all others to seeking bread abroad) came 'among those that came' (in the press of many foreigners, Canaanites and others, drawn to Egypt's stores, Joseph's wise storing making Egypt the granary of the nations); 'for the famine was in the land of Canaan' (the dearth reaching the land of promise, the heirs of the covenant not exempt from the common distress, driven to the same refuge).

themes: the sons of Israel came to buy
the heirs of promise in want

schools.summary: The fulfilment of the dreams — 'Joseph was the governor' (shallit, a ruler and protector over the land, esp. the corn; the title of the later Sultans, Clarke; he sold by deputy but so large a party drew his notice, Ellicott); 'Joseph's brethren bowed down with their faces to the earth' (the men who scorned his dreams of their sheaves and stars now prostrate before him, all unknowing, exactly fulfilling Gen 37:7,9 — God overruling their malice to accomplish the thing they resisted).
Calvin: Moses joins Joseph's high honour to his fidelity and diligence — he was raised because he was faithful.

themes: bowed down with their faces to the earth

schools.summary: Joseph's feigned harshness — he 'knew them' at once though they knew not him, yet 'made himself strange' (assumed the foreign stranger's manner) and 'spake roughly'; the expositors defending him — not revenge or bitterness but a higher design (Calvin), his aim to see Benjamin and prove his brothers to repentance (Ellicott); the rough speech a disguise of love, concealing his heart while he discovers whether they are changed; the puzzle (Henry, Wesley) why he never sent word to his father in twenty years.

The one-sided recognition — Joseph knew them, they knew not him; the reasons for their failure (Gill): some twenty-two years passed, the beardless youth now a bearded man, clothed as an Egyptian prince not a Hebrew shepherd-boy, speaking through an interpreter in Egyptian, holding the highest office (the last place to look for their lost, supposedly dead brother); every outward mark altered or concealed, while their faces were unmistakable to him; the disguise providential, giving him freedom to test them.

The remembered dreams and charge of spying — their bowing bringing to mind the boyhood visions of sheaves and stars (Gen 37:7,9), now fulfilled; Joseph remembering the dreams though they had forgotten, the laying up of God's oracles guiding his conduct toward their fulfilment (Wesley); 'ye are spies; to see the nakedness of the land' (meraggelim, those who scout a land's defences, Clarke; such suspicion fell on every Eastern traveller, the Semites especially distrusted in Egypt with its frontier fortresses, Ellicott); the charge a device to test and probe, not a sincere belief.

The brothers' humble denial — 'Nay, my lord' (spoken by one for all or each in turn, a flat denial of spying); their deference, calling him 'my lord' and themselves 'thy servants' (bowing in word as in body, unconsciously continuing to fulfil the dreams they derided); the plain defence 'to buy food are thy servants come' (an honest, ordinary errand in famine, no design against the land); genuine alarm beneath their courtesy, pressing their innocence with submission.

themes: to buy food are thy servants come

schools.summary: The plea of common parentage — 'we are all one man's sons' (not a band from different tribes for a hostile purpose but one family; unlikely a father would send all his sons as spies on so perilous an errand, risking his whole house, Clarke, Ellicott); a sound plea, their travelling together as brothers itself arguing an innocent domestic purpose; 'we are true men, thy servants are no spies' (flatly repudiating the charge); unwittingly disclosing the family particulars Joseph wishes to learn.

themes: we are all one man's sons

schools.summary: Joseph's persistence in the charge — 'Nay' (refusing their bare assertion of honesty, the family argument brushed aside); 'but to see the nakedness of the land ye are come' (insisting again they came to scout Egypt's weak places); the purpose (Gill) to drive them to a fuller account of themselves; if they offer no better proof than their word, he holds them to the charge, compelling them to disclose more of their family — leading them to reveal the youngest brother and the one who 'is not'; the severity calculated, each rebuff a step toward the truth about Benjamin.

The disclosure of the family — 'twelve brethren, the sons of one man in Canaan' (widening their plea, naming the full count, father, and homeland as their credential); the two absent — 'the youngest is this day with our father, and one is not'; 'one is not' an elliptical phrase, one is no longer alive (Clarke), they supposing Joseph dead; they say they 'are' twelve though one was thought gone, yet all twelve lived, the supposed-dead brother standing before them; unintentionally handing Joseph the knowledge he sought — that his full brother and father live.

Joseph turning their words against them — 'that is it that I spake unto you, saying, Ye are spies' (treating their account, far from clearing them, as confirming his suspicion); a pretext, for their disclosure (the youngest at home, one gone) is precisely what he wanted; his real aim (Ellicott) to get Benjamin into his power, to have his full brother brought down, the demand to produce the youngest his lever; as for the rest, no settled purpose yet, his plan unfolding step by step.

themes: that is it that I spake

schools.summary: The test and the oath — 'hereby ye shall be proved' (the test of honesty to be producing Benjamin, the demand falling precisely where Joseph's heart is set, on his full brother, under the guise of proving their veracity); 'by the life of Pharaoh ye shall not go forth... except your youngest brother come' (a solemn asseveration; swearing by the king's life an ancient custom, 1 Sam 17:55, Ellicott; 'as surely as Pharaoh lives,' the brothers reckoning it an oath, Clarke; Joseph speaking in his assumed Egyptian character).
Calvin raises the question whether Joseph did well to swear by Pharaoh's life, since the godly were taught to swear only by the name of God.

The proposed proof — 'send one of you, and let him fetch your brother' (a single brother dispatched to bring Benjamin), 'ye shall be kept in prison' (the rest held as hostages, securing the return); the purpose 'that your words may be proved, whether there be any truth in you' (producing the youngest the decisive test of veracity); the renewed oath 'by the life of Pharaoh surely ye are spies' (the charge confirmed if they fail); the device aimed beneath its severity at bringing Benjamin into Joseph's sight.

themes: ye shall be kept in prison
that your words may be proved

schools.summary: The three days' confinement — Joseph shows himself severe not by words only but by the act, shutting them all up as if about to punish, tormenting them with fear for three days (Calvin); the interval giving them time to consult who should fetch Benjamin, a matter none could settle, none willing to carry such evil tidings to their father (Gill); the confinement a real affliction, humbling them and working on their consciences, preparing the penitent reflection that follows.

Joseph's relenting and fear of God — on the third day, his heart yearning and finding they could not agree who should go, he draws back from his harsh order (Gill); 'This do, and live' (offering life and release on milder terms); 'for I fear God' (his conduct governed not by caprice but the fear of God who will not let him deal unjustly); by the name Elohim having them understand he worships the same God as they (Ellicott), a hint of who he is; the fear of God the restraint keeping his testing within mercy and justice.

The softened terms — instead of holding all but one, Joseph will detain only one and free the rest (Keil and Delitzsch); 'if ye be true men, let one of your brethren be bound' (a single hostage); the mercy 'go ye, carry corn for the famine of your houses' (the nine returning at once with grain that their starving households not perish; Joseph not letting famine slay the wider family while he tests the brothers); consideration for the helpless women and children; the proof still the bringing of Benjamin.

themes: one of your brethren be bound

schools.summary: The condition and the brothers' submission — 'bring your youngest brother unto me' (the producing of Benjamin the decisive proof); 'so shall your words be verified, and ye shall not die' (the test holding out life if they bring him); Joseph's twofold motive (Ellicott) — his longing to see his only full brother, and reckoning that having Benjamin is the surest means of drawing his aged father down to Egypt too; 'and they did so' (the brothers accepting the terms, submitting to the dreaded errand), setting the stage for the penitent reckoning.

themes: so shall your words be verified

schools.summary: The awakening of conscience — after some twenty-two years' silence, God so combines the circumstances (accusation, prison, helplessness) that the long-dulled conscience is roused (Clarke); before affliction they lay in torpor, now reduced to straits they recall the fratricide of years before (Calvin); the confession 'we are verily guilty concerning our brother... we saw the anguish of his soul, when he besought us, and we would not hear' (the scene at the pit returning to accuse them); the just inference 'therefore is this distress come upon us' (conscience reading providence); true penitence stirring (Wesley).

Reuben's reproach — 'Spake I not unto you... Do not sin against the child; and ye would not hear?' (Reuben, who had tried to save Joseph, recalling his unheeded warning, magnifying their fault for not listening to prudent counsel, Calvin); 'his blood is required' (reading their trouble as the demanded reckoning for Joseph's blood, the dread of bloodguilt pressing); the brothers plainly believing Joseph dead, so 'one is not' was no deliberate lie (Ellicott); the reproach sharpening the general remorse, conscience fully awake.

themes: do not sin against the child

schools.summary: The hidden hearer — 'they knew not that Joseph understood them' (speaking among themselves in Hebrew, taking the governor for a native Egyptian, never dreaming he could follow their words); 'for he spake unto them by an interpreter' (conducting the whole interview through a translator as though he knew no Hebrew, to conceal himself; either the tongues differed greatly or he deliberately affected ignorance, Clarke); the pathos — the very brother of whom they spoke heard every word of their remorse, the confession he longed for, moving him to weep.

themes: they knew not that Joseph understood

schools.summary: Joseph's tears and the binding of Simeon — hearing their confession and Reuben's concern, he 'turned about and wept' (no bitterness in his heart, melting at their first word of regret, Ellicott), then mastered his feelings and 'returned,' lest he lose the chance to see Benjamin; he 'took Simeon, and bound him before their eyes' (the hostage held in Egypt); the choice — by tradition Simeon bound Joseph and cast him in the pit, a fitting retribution (Clarke), or his most bitter enemy, now least humbled (Wesley); bound before their eyes to affect them all.

Joseph's secret generosity — the threefold provision: sacks filled with corn, money restored unseen into each man's sack, provision given for the way; mercy at work under the mask of harshness, returning the very price they paid while holding Simeon and demanding Benjamin; the 'sacks' leather-lined bags for transport (Clarke).

Henry: as Joseph gives out supplies without money and without price, so Christ dispenses the bread of life freely, inviting the poorest; yet guilty consciences are apt to misread good providences as bad omens — the returned money filling the awakened brothers with fresh terror.

themes: hidden grace, a type of Christ

schools.summary: The departure with the grain — 'they laded their asses with the corn, and departed' (the errand accomplished, their beasts loaded for the journey to Canaan); the scale (Clarke) — to feed so large a household as Jacob's, the asses must have numbered many, scores if not hundreds, the corn having to suffice for a great family through the famine; hidden in their sacks travels not only the corn but the returned money, a kindness they will soon misread; they go home burdened in body and conscience, a brother left bound behind.

themes: laded their asses with the corn
the hidden money travels with them

schools.summary: The discovery of the money — 'as one of them opened his sack to give his ass provender in the inn' (the lodging-place where the caravan halted, a recognized spot to pass the night, not a modern hostelry, Keil/Ellicott); 'he espied his money... in his sack's mouth' (the very price paid, restored); only this one opened his sack on the way and found it, the rest discovering theirs at home (Clarke); Joseph's intended kindness coming to the awakened brothers not as comfort but alarm, their stirred consciences reading the money as fresh danger.

themes: one of them opened his sack

schools.summary: Their terror and sense of God — 'my money is restored' (the discovery spreading dismay); 'their heart failed them' (Heb. 'their heart went out/forth,' the spasm of fear at sudden alarm, like 'the heart sinks,' Clarke; 'they trembled each to his brother,' Ellicott); knowing the Egyptians abhorred Hebrews and expecting no favour, they suspect the money a snare (Wesley); 'What is this that God hath done unto us?' (reading the event as God's act; perceiving God angry, deploring their wretchedness, Calvin) — the guilty conscience tracing God's hand in the alarming providence.

themes: what is this that God hath done

schools.summary: The report to Jacob — safe arrival 'unto Jacob their father unto the land of Canaan' (without the pursuit they feared); 'told him all that befell unto them' (the full account — suspected and accused as spies, threatened, imprisoned, Simeon kept in bonds, the youngest to be brought, Henry); the repetition that follows not superfluous (Calvin), showing with what anxiety they excused leaving Simeon and pleaded for Benjamin; the weight of the news on the aged, bereaved Jacob — Simeon gone, Benjamin demanded.

The beginning of the brothers' account — 'the man, who is the lord of the land' (not the king but the governor, the deputy under Pharaoh, whose authority reached all Egypt and especially the corn, Gill; to the brothers the supreme power they dealt with); 'spake roughly to us, and took us for spies' (reporting plainly the harsh reception and grave charge); the dramatic irony — the 'lord of the land' whose harshness they report is the father's own lost son, of whom they still speak as dead; opening the recital that justifies the demand for Benjamin.

themes: the man, the lord of the land

schools.summary: Their rehearsed defence — they recount to Jacob their reply to the governor, 'We are true men; we are no spies' (protesting their honesty and flatly denying the charge); upright men not given to treacherous or treasonable dealings, come with no hostile design but only to buy corn to relieve their starving families (Gill); the recital part of the careful account preparing Jacob for the hard demand; the continued irony — the 'him' to whom they made this defence is Joseph himself.

The family disclosed again — recounting to Jacob how they pleaded 'we be twelve brethren, sons of our father' (their common parentage as proof of an honest family, not spies); the unintended truth (Gill) — they say they 'be' twelve and in fact still were, though supposing one dead, all twelve yet living, the supposed-dead brother the very governor; 'one is not' (Joseph, believed gone) 'and the youngest is this day with our father' (Benjamin); leading up to the governor's demand that the youngest be brought.

The terms reported to Jacob — 'the lord of the country said... Hereby shall I know that ye are true men; leave one of your brethren here with me' (the test: one held, the rest sent home with corn, the youngest to be brought); their tact (Ellicott) — while owning the lord spoke 'hard things,' they do not mention Simeon was left in bonds, nor the harsher treatment, lest Jacob be afraid to send Benjamin; shaping the report to make the demand bearable, softening the danger to win their father's consent.

themes: the lord of the country said

schools.summary: The promised reward of obedience — 'bring your youngest brother unto me' (the one condition on which all depends); the threefold promise relayed to commend the demand — 'then shall I know that ye are no spies, but true men' (honesty proved, the charge withdrawn), 'I will deliver you Simeon' (the held brother restored, the family made whole), 'ye shall traffick in the land' (free to come, go, and buy corn through the famine); holding out to Jacob the gains compliance would secure, that he might consent to let Benjamin go.

themes: ye are no spies but true men
ye shall traffick in the land

schools.summary: The money found by all — 'as they emptied their sacks... every man's bundle of money was in his sack' (what one found at the inn, all now find at home, each man's price restored); Calvin's shrewd inference — it shows how greatly they had been alarmed on the journey, for had they been composed each would have examined his sack after money was found in one, betraying a mind robbed of presence of mind by fear; 'both they and their father... were afraid' (the alarm spreading to Jacob, the returned silver read as a snare, not a kindness).

Jacob's lament — 'me have ye bereaved of my children' (laying the loss at his sons' door, half-suspecting their hand, Gill); the tally 'Joseph is not, and Simeon is not, and ye will take Benjamin away' (one believed dead, one held, the youngest demanded); 'all these things are against me' (literally 'are upon me,' heavy burdens hastening him to the grave, Clarke/Ellicott; as though all evils fell on himself alone, Calvin); the poignant irony — he cries all is against him when all is secretly working for him; the misjudgment of grief that cannot see the providence.

themes: Joseph is not, and Simeon is not
all these things are against me

schools.summary: Reuben's rash pledge — 'Slay my two sons, if I bring him not to thee' (staking his own children's lives as surety for Benjamin's safe return); a strange proposal, yet showing the honesty and affection of Reuben's heart, feeling deeply his father's distress (Clarke); he does not suppose Jacob would really slay his grandchildren but offers the strongest assurance he can frame, reckoning the risk slight (Ellicott); 'deliver him into my hand, and I will bring him again' (taking the charge on himself); earnest but rash, ill-judged, what no father would accept.

Jacob's grief-stricken refusal — 'my son shall not go down with you' (a peremptory denial, implying distrust of his sons, Wesley); 'for his brother is dead, and he is left alone' (Joseph supposed dead, Benjamin the only remaining son of Rachel, the one treasure left); 'if mischief befall him... ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave' (the phrase of his old lament over Joseph, 'to the grave,' to Sheol, Gen 37:35, Ellicott; the image of the oppressive burden, Clarke); a lively picture of holy Jacob's oppression — not iron-hearted, yet his patience the more deserving (Calvin).

themes: my son shall not go down
his brother is dead, he is left alone
my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave
